metadata.title: Solve the Back-Door Criterion with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro.

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: The back-door criterion is the formal test for whether your adjustment set is valid. Not every set of variables you control for is safe. Controlling for a collider on a back-door path actually opens that path and introduces bias. And M-bias — where a collider sits between two independent causes — is the canonical trap. The question this video answers: how do you algorithmically check whether an adjustment set is valid or harmful.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'the back-door criterion — not every control variable is safe'; M-bias trap; no terminal yet

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The back-door criterion is the formal test for whether your adjustment set is valid. Not every set of variables you control for is safe. Controlling for a colli

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. Build a Python script using NetworkX that takes a DAG as an edge list plus treatment and outcome, finds all back-door paths, checks each candidate variable against the back-door criterion — it must block all back-door paths and contain no descendants of treatment — finds the minimal valid adjustment set, and calls Claude to explain any violations.

props.command: claude "Build a Python script using NetworkX: (1) takes DAG as edge list + treatment/outcome; (2) finds all back-door paths; (3) checks each candidate variable against back-door criterion (blocks all back-door paths AND no descendants of treatment); (4) finds minimal valid adjustment set; (5) calls Claude to explain violations"

beats.narration_text: Read it. The validity check is two conditions joined by AND. First: the set blocks all back-door paths — every path from treatment to outcome that contains an arrow into treatment must be blocked by some variable in the adjustment set. Second: no variable in the set is a descendant of treatment — adjusting for a descendant re-introduces collider bias through the back door.

props.code:
# backdoor_solver.py — Claude Code output

def check_adjustment_set(G, Z, X, Y):
    valid = (
        blocks_all_backdoor(G, Z, X, Y)
        and not contains_descendant(G, Z, X)
    )
    return valid

# M-bias DAG: A->X, A->M, B->M, B->Y, X->Y
# Z={M}: INVALID — M is a collider, conditioning opens A-B path
# Z={A}: VALID — blocks A->X back-door without touching descendants
# Z={B}: VALID — blocks B->Y back-door without touching descendants

beats.narration_text: Run it on the M-bias DAG. Five nodes: X treatment, Y outcome, A on the left, B on the right, M in the middle as a collider. Conditioning on M turns gold — invalid, because M is a collider between A and B, and conditioning on it opens the A-to-B path, which flows through to X and Y. Conditioning on A alone or B alone turns green — valid adjustment sets. Either one blocks a back-door path without opening a collider.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: M-bias DAG animated, M node highlighted as invalid collider in gold, A and B highlighted as valid adjustment sets in green

beats.narration_text: Now ask Claude to extend the solver to find the minimal adjustment set — the smallest valid set — and to handle the case where no valid set exists. Some DAGs have no valid back-door adjustment; in those cases you need the front-door criterion or instrumental variables.

props.command:
claude "update backdoor_solver.py:
  -> find minimal valid adjustment set
  -> handle case where no valid set exists
  -> suggest front-door or IV alternative"

beats.narration_text: The updated solver now searches all subsets of observed variables, returns the minimal valid adjustment set, and when no back-door adjustment exists, prints which criterion to try next. For the M-bias DAG it returns A or B — both minimal and valid — and notes that M is never admissible.
beats.visual_intent: slate: terminal output showing minimal adjustment set search results and alternative suggestions

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The updated solver now searches all subsets of observed variables, returns the minimal valid adjustment set, and when no back-door adjustment exists, prints whi

beats.narration_text: So the back-door criterion is a two-part test. Block all back-door paths. Touch no descendants. A variable that satisfies both is admissible. A variable that is a collider on a back-door path violates the first condition in the worst possible way — it unblocks a path that was already closed.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: two conditions for back-door validity; M-bias as the canonical counterexample

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So the back-door criterion is a two-part test. Block all back-door paths. Touch no descendants. A variable that satisfies both is admissible. A variable that is

beats.narration_text: Your move: draw the DAG for your study domain and run the back-door solver. Check every variable you were planning to control for. If any of them are colliders on a back-door path, conditioning on them will hurt, not help. The next reel covers randomization — the one intervention that eliminates all back-door paths at once.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run solver on your study DAG, 2) check for colliders in your adjustment set, 3) proceed to randomization reel

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Your move: draw the DAG for your study domain and run the back-door solver. Check every variable you were planning to control for. If any of them are colliders

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
